title: Nothing Arises Alone

paragraphs: The early Buddhist tradition, particularly the strand that took shape in the centuries after the Buddha's death in northern India in the fifth century BCE, returned repeatedly to a single doctrine that the surrounding philosophical world found difficult and that the subsequent two and a half thousand years of Buddhist thought have continued to elaborate, defend, and dispute. The doctrine is at once cosmological, ethical, psychological, and ontological. It proposes that nothing in the universe arises by itself, that every event and every entity depends for its existence on a particular configuration of conditions, and that the apparent independence and substantiality of any single thing — including the self — is an artefact of a certain way of looking, not a feature of how things actually are.

Pratītyasamutpāda is the Sanskrit name for the doctrine; in Pali, the language of the earliest preserved Buddhist canon, it is paṭiccasamuppāda. The compound is often translated as 'dependent origination' or 'dependent arising,' though both translations leave considerable work to the reader. The doctrine has, in different schools of Buddhist thought, been formalised as a twelve-link chain of causation, as a more general principle of co-arising without strict temporal ordering, and as an analytic tool for revealing the absence of independent self-nature in any phenomenon whatsoever. The differences among these formulations are substantial and have produced serious philosophical debate across the entire history of Buddhist thought, but the underlying intuition is shared: things do not exist alone, and to understand any single thing requires understanding the conditions in which it arises.

What is distinctive about pratītyasamutpāda, considered against the European philosophical tradition, is its refusal of the substantial individual as the basic unit of analysis. The dominant Western metaphysical tradition since at least Aristotle has tended to treat individual substances as primary and relations between them as secondary; the Buddhist tradition has tended to treat the relations as primary and the individuals as derivative — not unreal, exactly, but real only as nodes in the network of conditions that produced them. The self, on this account, is not a soul or a substance but a pattern, continuously re-arising on the basis of the conditions that sustain it, and dissolving when the conditions change. The implications for ethics, for psychology, and for political theory are substantial and have been worked out in different ways across the Buddhist intellectual traditions of South, Southeast, Central, and East Asia.

The contemporary scientific understanding of complex systems — ecology, neuroscience, ecology, evolutionary biology, network theory — has, in different vocabulary, been working out something structurally similar to the Buddhist insight across the past century. The organism does not exist independently of its ecological niche; the ecological niche does not exist independently of the organisms that constitute it; the brain does not exist independently of the body it is embedded in or the social environment that organises its inputs; the gene does not exist independently of the cellular machinery that reads it. Each of these is a small contemporary scientific version of the older Buddhist insight, conducted in the laboratory rather than in the meditation hall, and arriving at conclusions that the philosophical tradition of substances and properties has substantially struggled to accommodate.

What pratītyasamutpāda asks of a person who takes it seriously, in any of its formulations, is a particular kind of attention to the conditions that have produced the present moment. The person, the room, the conversation, the feeling, the obligation, the opportunity — each is not a fact that simply is, but a node in a network of conditions whose visibility depends on the depth and breadth of the attention brought to it. The practice of attending to the conditions, rather than to the apparent solidity of the resulting entities, is one of the central exercises of Buddhist meditation across its many schools, and is offered not as a metaphysical exercise for its own sake but as a practical training in seeing the world more accurately than the default mode of perception permits.

What remains, considered at the close of a month in which a substantial portion of the world's languages have been asked to give up small fragments of their unique vocabulary, is the recognition that the words themselves were never independent things. Each word in this month's series arose in particular conditions — historical, cultural, linguistic, individual — and exists in its full sense only as a node in the network of those conditions. The borrowed word lifted out of its surroundings is not quite the word in its native life; the curated collection of borrowed words assembled here is not the lived experience of any of the cultures the words came from. What such a collection can do is point at the territory the words named, in the cultures where they did their original work, and invite the reader who has been borrowing them to attend more carefully to the conditions of their own life that the borrowed words have made briefly visible. The work of pratītyasamutpāda is to keep that attention going.
essay.blockquote: Things do not exist alone — and the practice of attending to the conditions that sustain any single thing is the slow, patient work the doctrine asks of anyone willing to take it seriously.

positions: Pratītyasamutpāda offers a substantially more accurate metaphysics than the European tradition of individual substances and properties, and the convergence between the Buddhist insight and contemporary complex-systems science suggests that the doctrine is not a piece of religious mysticism but a piece of careful descriptive philosophy that the European tradition has spent two and a half thousand years catching up with. To take the doctrine seriously is to recover a philosophical resource that has been substantially neglected in the Western philosophical mainstream.

The contemporary Western reception of pratītyasamutpāda, particularly in its popularised mindfulness and 'interconnectedness' forms, has substantially flattened the doctrine's actual philosophical demandingness into a vague feeling of relational openness that the original Buddhist analytic tradition would have found insufficient. The doctrine's full working through requires sustained engagement with technical philosophical questions — about causation, about identity over time, about the relationship between conventional and ultimate truth — that the popular use rarely undertakes, and the appropriation of the slogan without the analytic substance has done both the doctrine and its appropriators less service than it appears to have done.

number.label: Number of links in the most fully developed classical formulation of pratītyasamutpāda — the nidana chain that traces the conditioned arising of suffering from ignorance through volitional formations, consciousness, name-and-form, the six sense bases, contact, feeling, craving, clinging, becoming, birth, and old-age-and-death, with each link conditioning the next in a cycle that the practice is meant to interrupt.

fragment: He had been keeping the notebook for thirty-six years. The early entries were in handwriting he no longer entirely recognised as his own; the later ones, on the laptop, in a voice he could still hear in his head when he read them back. The continuity between the boy who had begun the notebook and the man who was now closing it had been, on careful examination, less a continuity of self than a continuity of attention: the same patient looking, conducted across decades by a series of slightly different people each of whom had inherited the notebook from the previous one and added a few entries of their own. The notebook had been the network. The selves had been the nodes. He set it down on the desk and turned out the light.

word.origin: Sanskrit; from the Pali 'paṭiccasamuppāda,' the earlier form preserved in the Theravāda Buddhist canon. The compound is composed of 'pratītya' (depending on, contingent upon), 'sam' (together with, fully), and 'utpāda' (arising, appearing). Literally: 'arising-together-in-dependence,' though no single English compound captures the relational and processual character of the original. The doctrine is among the earliest distinctively Buddhist philosophical claims, attested in the foundational texts of the Pali canon (the Saṃyutta Nikāya, the Majjhima Nikāya) and elaborated across substantially all subsequent Buddhist scholastic traditions, including the Madhyamaka analyses of Nāgārjuna in the second century CE and the extensive Yogācāra and East Asian developments of the following centuries.
word.definition: The Buddhist doctrine that nothing in the universe arises independently — that every event and every entity depends for its existence on a particular configuration of conditions, and that the apparent independence and substantiality of any single phenomenon is an artefact of a certain way of looking rather than a feature of how things actually are. Often translated as 'dependent origination' or 'dependent arising,' though both translations leave considerable work to the reader.

quote.text: इदं अस्मिन् सति भवति, इदं अस्मिन् असति न भवति।
quote.translation: When this is, that is; when this is not, that is not.
quote.author: Anonymous (early Buddhist canonical text)
quote.source: Samyutta Nikāya, 12.21 (Pali canon, compiled c. 1st century BCE)

on_this_day.place: Tunguska region, Krasnoyarsk Krai, Russian Empire (present-day Siberia, Russian Federation)
on_this_day.text: On 30 June 1908, at approximately 7:17 in the morning local time, an atmospheric explosion of energy equivalent to approximately 10 megatons of TNT occurred over the Stony Tunguska River in central Siberia, flattening an estimated 80 million trees across an area of more than 2,000 square kilometres but causing no documented human casualties because of the extreme remoteness of the location. The Tunguska event is the largest impact event in recorded human history, almost certainly caused by the atmospheric airburst of a small asteroid or comet fragment, and the absence of an impact crater on the ground has continued to fuel investigation across the following century. The event illustrates, in unusually pure form, the doctrine the essay describes: a small piece of solar-system debris, present in the inner solar system through the conditioning history of stellar nucleosynthesis and planetary formation, intersected the orbit of the Earth at the precise conjunction of conditions that produced the airburst, which itself produced the forest destruction, which itself produced the long subsequent history of investigation. Nothing in the chain was independent of any other link, and the whole event is unintelligible without the full network of conditions that produced it.

person.name: Nāgārjuna
person.dates: c. 150 – c. 250 CE · South India / Andhra Pradesh, India
paragraphs: Nāgārjuna was the second-century Indian Buddhist philosopher whose Mūlamadhyamakakārikā (Fundamental Verses on the Middle Way) is among the most important single philosophical works in the entire Buddhist tradition and one of the most rigorously argued texts in the history of South Asian philosophy. The work develops a series of dialectical arguments designed to demonstrate the emptiness (śūnyatā) of all phenomena — the absence of any independent self-nature in anything whatsoever — through a thoroughgoing application of the principle of pratītyasamutpāda to every category of thought the surrounding philosophical traditions had relied on. The Madhyamaka school Nāgārjuna founded became one of the two principal Mahāyāna Buddhist philosophical traditions and has had enormous influence across Tibet, China, Korea, Japan, and Vietnam, with sustained scholarly engagement continuing to the present day.

Nāgārjuna's complication, biographically, is the substantial uncertainty about almost everything historical concerning him: his dates, his birthplace, his teachers, his patrons, his relationship to other texts that have been attributed to him over the centuries. The philosophical work survives in substantial and reliable textual form; the man behind the work is largely unrecoverable, and what is reported about him in the various Buddhist hagiographical traditions is a layered accumulation of legend that the modern critical scholarship has had limited success in disentangling. The pattern is familiar from many foundational ancient philosophers — Socrates, Confucius, the historical Buddha himself — whose intellectual achievements have continued to be argued about for two thousand years while the biographical persons who produced them have receded into a haze the available sources cannot dispel.

now_here.name: Nalanda Mahavihara Ruins
now_here.text: The ruins of what was, between the fifth and the twelfth centuries CE, one of the largest residential universities in the pre-modern world — a Buddhist monastic complex of perhaps ten thousand monks and a thousand teachers at its peak, drawing students from across South, Southeast, and East Asia and serving as one of the principal centres of Buddhist philosophical scholarship during the long period when the Madhyamaka, Yogācāra, and other Mahāyāna schools were being developed and elaborated. The site was sacked in approximately 1193 by the Ghurid general Bakhtiyar Khilji, and the surviving library — said to have contained hundreds of thousands of manuscripts — burned for months. The ruins, excavated since the late nineteenth century and now administered as an archaeological park, preserve the foundations of the monasteries, lecture halls, and temple complexes; the modern Nalanda University, founded in 2014 on a nearby site, is an attempt by the Indian government and a consortium of Asian states to revive, in a contemporary form, the tradition of cross-cultural Asian scholarship that the original Nalanda represented across seven centuries.

questions.text: Looking back across this month of words from twenty-two language traditions, which of them has most genuinely entered your working vocabulary — not as an exotic decoration but as a category you now use to organise your perception of your own life?
questions.deeper: What allowed that particular word to take root where others slipped through? And what does the answer tell you about the conditions under which a borrowed concept actually becomes a working part of how a person thinks?
questions.experiment: Pick the one word from this month that has most affected you. Use it deliberately at least three times in conversation or in writing in the coming month, with people who will not know it. Notice both what the use enables and what the word loses each time it is offered out of its original context.

questions.text: The doctrine of pratītyasamutpāda proposes that nothing arises alone. If you applied this analytic to your own current life — to the present configuration of your work, your relationships, your physical condition, your inner weather — which of the conditions that produced the current configuration are most visible to you, and which are most invisible?
questions.deeper: Are the invisible conditions invisible because they are genuinely subtle or because you have been trained not to attend to them? And what would it require to make the most consequential invisible conditions visible enough to be considered?
questions.experiment: Pick one element of your present life — a habit, a relationship, a recurring feeling, a professional position — and spend an hour mapping, as completely as you can, the network of conditions that have produced and sustain it. Do not aim for a comprehensive map; aim only to extend the visibility of the conditions further than your default perception extends it. Notice what becomes apparent.

questions.text: The essay closes by noting that the words themselves were never independent things — that each arose in particular conditions and exists in its full sense only as a node in the network of those conditions. How has this month of attending to small specific words from many traditions changed your relation to your own native language?
questions.deeper: Has the exposure to vocabularies that handle aspects of inner life differently from your own native language made you more aware of what your own language does well, what it does poorly, and what it does not attempt at all? And what would you do with the awareness now that you have it?
questions.experiment: Take one element of your inner life that your native language handles poorly and consider whether it is worth attempting, in writing or in conversation, a sustained articulation of it — using either a borrowed word, a coined one, or an extended periphrasis that takes the time the native lexical resources do not supply. Conduct the articulation this week. Notice both what becomes possible to think and to say, and what limits even the deliberate articulation cannot quite cross.

trivia.question: The Madhyamaka school of Buddhist philosophy, founded by Nāgārjuna in approximately the second century CE, is one of the two principal philosophical schools of Mahāyāna Buddhism. What is the other?
trivia.explanation: The two principal philosophical schools of Mahāyāna Buddhism are Madhyamaka (founded by Nāgārjuna in the second century CE) and Yogācāra (founded by Asaṅga and Vasubandhu in the fourth century CE). The two schools developed substantially different philosophical analyses of the doctrine of dependent origination and of the nature of mind and reality, and the centuries of debate between them produced much of the most sophisticated philosophical literature in the broader Buddhist tradition. Theravāda is a separate major branch of Buddhism with its own distinct philosophical traditions; Vajrayāna is a third major branch incorporating tantric practice; and Zen is an East Asian school within the broader Mahāyāna umbrella, not itself a philosophical school in the technical sense Madhyamaka and Yogācāra are.

data.definition: The condition of mutual reliance among entities such that none can be adequately understood in isolation from the others — the contemporary English term that comes closest to the territory the Buddhist doctrine of pratītyasamutpāda has articulated for two and a half thousand years, though the English word carries substantially less philosophical weight than the original and has been substantially flattened in its popular contemporary use into a vague slogan of relational openness.

movie.title: Why Has Bodhi-Dharma Left for the East?
movie.text: A South Korean film made over seven years by a painter-turned-filmmaker, set in a remote mountain Zen monastery where an old monk, his young disciple, and an orphaned boy live in extended seasonal solitude. The film operates on a temporal register that few other works in any medium attempt: long held shots of natural processes — water flowing over stones, fog moving through a valley, the slow progress of a small wound on the orphaned boy's hand — that ask the viewer to attend to the patient unfolding of conditions rather than to any narrative event. The film is among the few works of cinema that approach the doctrine the essay describes from inside its working assumptions, treating the present moment as a node in a network of conditions whose visibility is the proper subject of attention.
